title: FACT SHEET: Treasury Department Releases First-Of-Its-Kind Report on Benefits of Unions to the U.S. Economy
summary: The Treasury Department released a comprehensive report by the Office of Economic Policy examining the economic role of labor unions, finding that unions raise member wages by 10-15% and contribute to middle-class financial stability. The report, part of over 70 actions by the White House Task Force on Worker Organizing and Empowerment, discusses spillover effects on nonunion workers and broader economic resilience.
key_points: Unions raise wages of their members by approximately 10-15% according to the report
Workers at nonunionized firms also see increased wages due to competitive spillover effects
Union representation rate for Black men was 13% in 2021, compared to a population average of 10%
Union election petitions in 2022 reached their highest level since 2015, indicating labor movement revitalization
The report is part of the Biden-Harris Administration's broader Worker Organizing and Empowerment Task Force agenda
Public opinion of labor unions is at its highest level in over 50 years according to the report
why_it_matters: This Treasury report provides economic research supporting unionization but does not constitute a market-moving catalyst. It reflects the Biden-Harris Administration's policy stance on labor issues rather than presenting new economic data or policy changes that would immediately affect financial markets. Relevant for understanding long-term labor market policy direction and potential legislative priorities like the PRO Act.
macro_context: The report discusses labor economics in macroeconomic terms, noting that unions can reduce inequality (which can affect growth by enabling better resource allocation), boost productivity through improved working conditions, and contribute to economic resilience through middle-class stability. However, this is policy research, not a macro data release or central bank action.
important_numbers: $35 million threshold for project labor agreements via Executive Order 14063
